texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two novel hollow-fibre liquid -liquid membrane contactor (HF-LLMC) modules, containing S-type (skin layer with low porosity) and Q-type (skin layer with higher porosity) fibres, have been evaluated. Both fibres, with asymmetric, porous, and hydrophobic membranes, made from poly(4-methyl-1-pentene) (PMP), were used as efficient technology for ammonia recovery to produce liquid fertilisers. The ammonia-rich stream was fed into the shell side of the PMP-HF-LLMC, while nitric acid or phosphoric acid were fed separately into the lumen to produce N-type (NO3 --NH4 + ) and N-P-type (NH4 + -P2O5) fertilisers, respectively. The maximum NH3  recovery ( > 95%) was achieved in a closed-loop configuration with S-type fibres, while Q-type fibres showed a better performance in terms of the production of more concentrated N-type liquid fertiliser. With Q-type fibres, the highest  values  of  N-P-type  liquid  fertilisers  were  achieved  (8.0  %  N  (NH4 + )  and  20.3%  P2O5  (w/w))  using phosphoric acid, while the highest value of water flux across the PMP fibres was < 0.01 kg m -2 h 1 . The highest overall  mass  transfer  coefficient  (Km),  measured  for  solutions  containing  5.0 gNH3 L -1 with  a  feed/stripping volume ratio of 60:1, was (2.9 ± 0.2) × 10 7  ms 1 .  Additionally,  ultraviolet -visible spectroscopy and twodimensional excitation-emission  matrix  fluorescence  spectroscopy  were  employed  to  monitor  the  absence  of pore-wetting events and the stability of the PMP-HF-LLMC under strongly acidic and basic conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among various types of membrane processes, hollow-fibre liquid--liquid membrane contactors (HF-LLMCs) have been recently postulated as  an  innovative  method  for  NH3  recovery  [14 -20].  Membrane contactors act as a semi-permeable barrier between two distinct phases -liquid -liquid  in  the  case  of  HF-LLMCs  or  liquid -gas  for  hollow-fibre gas -liquid  membrane  contactors  (HF-LGMCs) -and  present  a  high ratio  of  interfacial  area  to  contactor  volume  (A/V  ratio).  The  most prominent advantage of hydrophobic HF-LLMCs is their ability to produce a dispersion-free contact. This separates two phases with a large and stable interfacial area, and controls independently the velocities of both phases, avoiding flooding or unloading [21]. To maximise their efficiency and performance, different compact configurations allowing changes in feed flow regime (e.g., tangential, radial, transverse or parallel) provide the highest A/V ratios [22]. Among these configurations, the type of external feed flow involving a cross-flow configuration has the characteristic that the throughput per membrane area increases, and the contactor is thus more compact [23]. The basis of the separation mechanism  in  HF-LLMCs,  when  treating  aqueous  solutions,  is  the transfer of solutes in gas form (e.g., NH3(g)) between two phases, taking advantage of the hydrophobic properties of the membrane material. The solute  transport  between  feed  and  stripping  side  fluids  is  obtained through  nondispersive  contact  via  the  microporous  structure  of  the membrane, based on a complex set of processes where diffusion within the membrane microporosity is the main mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the case of NH3 mass transfer in aqueous solutions, it evaporates from the feed solution (typically at basic pH values of > 11) to the gas phase contained in the membrane porosity, diffuses within the pores of the membrane and dissolves in the aqueous receiving phase (stripping solution) at the permeate side. Typically, this final stage is enhanced by the chemical reaction of the dissolved NH3 with strong inorganic acids such as H3PO4, HNO3 and/or H2SO4. As a result, NH3 is converted into ammonium ions (NH4 + ) producing concentrated streams of ammonium nitrate  (NH4NO3),  ammonium sulfate  ((NH4)2SO4),  ammonium dihydrogen phosphate ((NH4)H2PO4) or diammonium phosphate ((NH4)2HPO4), among others, which can be used as liquid fertilisers [24 -28]. This process can be conducted in one-through or recirculation modes.  High  removal  and  recovery  rates  are  the  advantages  of  this recycling mode, although one-through mode designs are more straightforward [29].

texts.orig: [30,31]. Polyolefins have been found in the last decade to be the most suitable for making HFs due to their chemically and mechanically stable structure  and economical availability. In addition, their hydrophobic nature is a suitable choice for membrane contactor applications destined for transport in gas form because it significantly hinders pore wetting [32]. For many decades, polypropylene (PP) has been widely used to produce HF-LLMCs, while at a smaller scale, polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) polymers have been employed, although rarely, due to their higher cost [33,34]. One of the promising and desirable polymers of the polyolefin family is poly(4methyl-1-pentene)  (PMP),  which  has  recently  been  applied  in  HFGLMCs  in  the  field  of  degasification  of  aqueous  solutions  (e.g.,  O2, CO2) due to their hydrophobic nature, low energy of intermolecular interactions,  low  bulk  density,  good  chemical  resistance,  inertness, biocompatibility,  and  similar  mechanical  and  structural  properties [35 -37]. As a thermoplastic material, PMP can be processed via melt technologies using green solvents [38], and a hollow-fibre membrane contactor (HF-LLMC) based on PMP can be produced using the nonreagent extrusion method [39]. Despite its use in gas separation, due to  its  semi-crystalline  structure,  adjustable  permeability  and  reliable chemical and mechanical stability, the thick selective layer of HFs made from  PMP  polymers  have  not  found  extensive  applications  [39 -41]. Moreover,  the  presence  of  methyl  side-chains  in  the  PMP  molecules prevents  the  formation  of  hydrogen  bonds  between  water  and  the membrane  surface,  thereby  improving  its  hydrophobicity.  Several companies, such as Mitsui Chemical Inc. (Japan) under the trademark TPX,  Dainippon  Ink  and  Chemicals  Co.  (DIC)  under  the  trademark Separel, and DuPont Water Solutions (USA) under the trademark Ligasep ™ are commercialising manufactured PMP-HF membranes, with a new asymmetric structure concept, for industrial and bench-scale applications [40,42 -44]. As can be seen in Fig. 1, DIC Co. (Japan) has developed a proprietary porous/non-porous membrane equipped with a surface  skin  layerstructure  of  1 µ m  supported  onto  a  more  porous structure of 30 -40 µ m for degasification enhancement. In fact, the main difrencess can be related to the porosity of the skin layer. The membrane A60 consisted of a porous skin layer with low porosity (around 30%), while the membrane Q-A60 has the same structure with higher porosity content of skin layer.  On the other  side, the  last  type  comprises  the porous support layer and then a dense skin layer without porosity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The main target of the current study was to evaluate the efficiency of recovering  NH3  from  treated  domestic  wastewater  to  produce  liquid fertiliser  using  two  HF-LLMC  modules  incorporating  hydrophobic asymmetric PMP fibres with a surface skin structure. To the best of our knowledge, no study has ever reported using such HF-LLMCs made from PMP for the recovery of NH3 from aqueous solutions, which highlights the novelty of the present study. In this regard, the effects of membrane properties (e.g., surface skin structure) and major operating parameters (e.g., the initial NH3  concentration in the feed stream, flow rate, and type  of  acidic  stripping  solution)  were  investigated.  Additionally, ultraviolet -visible (UV -Vis) spectroscopy and two-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (2D-EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy were used to determine membrane stability under the strongly acidic and basic conditions used in the modules as well as pore-wetting events.

In this regard, the effects of membrane properties (e.g., surface skin structure) and major operating parameters (e.g., the initial NH3  concentration in the feed stream, flow rate, and type  of  acidic  stripping  solution)  were  investigated.  Additionally, ultraviolet -visible (UV -Vis) spectroscopy and two-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (2D-EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy were used to determine membrane stability under the strongly acidic and basic conditions used in the modules as well as pore-wetting events.

texts.orig: To  investigate  NH3  transfer  through  the  PMP-HF-LLMC,  suitable amounts of NH3  were dissolved in domestic tap water to prepare NH3 synthetic solutions. Two commercial bench-scale PMP-HF-LLMC modules, named by the DIC Co. as perfusion modules since they are typically are used for the removal of gases from aqueous streams (Separel, DIC Corporation, Japan), were used. The properties of the two modules used are listed in Table 1 and details on the morphology structure are provided in Fig. 2. As can bee seen in this figure, SEM micrographs show the hollow fiber membrane with porous surface skin layer in which the main difference  is  associated  to  their  porousity  content  (S  type  skin  layer porousity is lower than that of for Q type).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. NH3 transport through PMP-HF-LLMC modules
texts.text: 2.4. NH3 transport through PMP-HF-LLMC modules

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The transport of gas molecules such as NH3  from feed to stripping side through a hydrophobic membrane can take place in distinct steps [50]. First, NH3  molecules transfer from the bulk feed solution to the feed/membrane interface through the mass boundary layer. In the next step, an equilibrium is established between the NH3 solution and the gas phase in the membrane pores. Then, NH3  molecules transfer from the feed/membrane interface to the membrane/stripping solution interface and  react  with  acid  at  the  permeate  side.  Finally,  the  salts  formed transfer through the boundary layer into the bulk stripping solution. The NH3 molecules in the feed solution tend to transform to NH3(g) because of the pH level of the feed stream, which is between 12 and 13. Therefore, the PMP-HF-LLMC, with a selective hydrophobic surface, allows that the NH3  molecules to diffuse through the pores and channels in gaseous form because of the higher NH3 partial pressure on the feed side (pNH3, f) compared to that on the acid stripping side (pNH3, f ≫ pNH3, s)  acting  as  a  driving  force,  while  other  ions  and  molecules  will  be repelled from the surface of the membrane. Following the diffusion of NH3  molecules, they react with the acid stripping phase and are converted  to  NH4 + ions.  Complete  conversion  of  NH3  to  NH4 + in  the stripping solution can only occur when the acidity is maintained below certain pH values. Details of the transport mechanism are detailed in section S1 of the supplementary information.

texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the current study, the effects of membrane characteristics on NH3 removal, mass transfer coefficient, liquid fertiliser formation, as well as NH3  and water fluxes were investigated using two different PMP-HFLLMC modules. To further compare the NH3  recovery performance of the A60 (S-type) and Q-A60 (Q-type) modules and in order to determine which  module  performs  more  efficient,  experiments  were  conducted with  initial  feed  concentrations  ranging  from  4.0  to  5.0  gNH3 L -1 . Otherwise, both feed and acid flow rates were kept constant at 450 mL min 1 , and HNO3  was chosen rather than H3PO4  as acid stripping solution as a result of its higher performance evaluated in a previous study [52]. The variation in NH3  concentration and its removal versus operation time in the feed solution are plotted in Fig. 4 (a) .
texts.text: In the current study, the effects of membrane characteristics on NH3 removal, mass transfer coefficient, liquid fertiliser formation, as well as NH3  and water fluxes were investigated using two different PMP-HFLLMC modules. To further compare the NH3  recovery performance of the A60 (S-type) and Q-A60 (Q-type) modules and in order to determine which  module  performs  more  efficient,  experiments  were  conducted with  initial  feed  concentrations  ranging  from  4.0  to  5.0  gNH3 L -1 . Otherwise, both feed and acid flow rates were kept constant at 450 mL min 1 , and HNO3  was chosen rather than H3PO4  as acid stripping solution as a result of its higher performance evaluated in a previous study [52]. The variation in NH3  concentration and its removal versus operation time in the feed solution are plotted in Fig. 4 (a) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 4 (a) , the NH3 concentration in the feed solution was reduced more rapidly by the Q-A60 than by the A60 module. Thus, NH3 concentration  in  the  feed  tank  reduced  gradually  from  4.3  to  0.2 gNH3 L -1 over  ~  74  h,  giving  ~  96%  NH3 recovery  using  the  A60 module. Notably, the final removal ratio was obtained when the NH3 concentration in the feed tank reached the steady-state value of around 0.2 gNH3 L -1 . On the other hand, using the Q-A60 module resulted in ~ 90% removal, reducing the NH3 feed concentration sharply from 4.9 to 0.7 gNH3 L -1 in one-third less operating time than that needed by the A60 module. Fig. 4 (a) clearly illustrates that almost 50% of NH3  recovery took place in the first 15 h for the A60 module, while the Q-A60 reached the same rate in only 8 h, which can be related to the different structures  of  the  skin  membrane  layer  of  the  PMP-HFs.  The  higher porosity of the surface skin layer of Q-A60 HFs provided higher NH3

texts.orig: The  overall  mass  transfer  coefficients  for  both  modules  were compared. The value of Km was obtained by linear regression of ln(C0/ Ct) versus time (t) (Fig. 4 (b) ). The value of Km shows the effectiveness of the membrane and stripping solution in transporting NH3 per area unit of the module. Screening the results, it is clear that the Q-A60 module showed a sharper increasing trend than did the A60 module. Km values of Q-A60 and A60 modules were (2.1 ± 0.1) × 10 7 and (1.0 ± 0.1) × 10 7  ms 1 ,  respectively; the value for Q-A60 was twice that of A60 (Table 2). One of the reasons for the slower NH3  transfer in the A60 module compared to the Q-A60 module might be its smaller pore size [53]. Other than the morphological features of the PMP membranes, the physical  characteristics  of  the  modules  have  great  effects  on  solute transport [54]. In this case, increasing the number of fibres increases the NH3  transport  due  to  the  higher  surface-to-volume  ratio  and  consequently higher production of N-liquid fertiliser.

texts.orig: In addition to NH3, H2O was transported across the hydrophobic membrane because of the water vapour pressure gradient. The water flux increases continuously over time, related to the greater water vapour pressure difference with the reduction of NH3 concentration  on  the  feed  side  and  the  increase  in  NH3  concentration  on  the permeate side. As Equation S7 describes, as the concentration of the ammonium salt  increases  in  the  stripping  stream,  the  water  activity coefficient ( γ w) and the mole fraction of water (xw) decrease and then the water vapour pressure between the feed and the stripping streams increases, and thus the water transport increases. It should be noted that water flux values measured were in inverse proportion to the NH3 flux. The  key  performance  indicators  of  both  modules  are  summarised  in Table 2.

texts.text: 3.1.1. Effect of stripping flow rate

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall response of the PMP-HF-LLMC system under the effect of different parameters was assessed in terms of NH3  uptake and, consequently,  fertiliser  production.  Experiments  were  carried  out  at  two different stripping flow rates (450 and 770 mL min 1 ) under constant conditions (feed flow rate set at 450 mL min 1 , acid stripping concentrations 0.4 M HNO3) to determine the influence of acid circuit flow rate in the expected HF membrane contactor operation range at laboratory scale. Fig. 5 (a) shows the reduction in NH3  concentration in the feed stream and its removal rate versus time. The NH3 concentration profiles followed a decreasing trend with the increase in acid flow rates; however, when the flow rate increased from 450 to 770 mL min 1 , the NH3 concentration showed a moderately sharper decline. Also, both experiments took 26 h with the same initial feed concentration and finally reached steady states of 0.7 and 0.3 gNH3 L -1 for 450 and 770 mL min 1 flow rates, respectively. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) an acid flow rate of 770 mL min 1 resulted in higher NH3  removal compared to at 450 mL min 1 and reached > 93% removal (almost 6% increase).

Experiments  were  carried  out  at  two different stripping flow rates (450 and 770 mL min 1 ) under constant conditions (feed flow rate set at 450 mL min 1 , acid stripping concentrations 0.4 M HNO3) to determine the influence of acid circuit flow rate in the expected HF membrane contactor operation range at laboratory scale. Fig. 5 (a) shows the reduction in NH3  concentration in the feed stream and its removal rate versus time. The NH3 concentration profiles followed a decreasing trend with the increase in acid flow rates; however, when the flow rate increased from 450 to 770 mL min 1 , the NH3 concentration showed a moderately sharper decline. Also, both experiments took 26 h with the same initial feed concentration and finally reached steady states of 0.7 and 0.3 gNH3 L -1 for 450 and 770 mL min 1 flow rates, respectively. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) an acid flow rate of 770 mL min 1 resulted in higher NH3  removal compared to at 450 mL min 1 and reached > 93% removal (almost 6% increase).

These results are in good agreement with those previously reported with PP-HF-LLMCs [2,16].

texts.orig: It  can  be  noted  that  variation  in  acid  flow  rate  may  have  both negative and positive impacts on the overall efficiency of an PMP-HFLLMC, since that depends on other factors such as working settings, initial feed concentration and membrane materials and/or configuration [2]. In this case, previous studies [20,34] reported that changes in acid stripping flow rate had a negligible effect on NH3 recovery; however, the mass transfer coefficient changed considerably.

After 2 h both fluxes reached same value and then showed similar JNH3 values for the rest of the experiment. In the case of Jw, an increase in acid stripping flow rate resulted in higher water  flux.  Meanwhile,  both  trends  regarding  the  acid  flow  rates reached a steady state after 20 h of experiment. It is noteworthy that the JNH3  and Jw for both flow rates showed reverse proportional trends. Overall, by increasing the acid flow rate the NH3 flux, NH3 removal and recovery percentage also increased. This observation could be attributed to the impact of flow turbulence resulting from the tangential flow of the stripping solution to the internal surface of the HFs that reduced the mass transfer boundary layer thickness. Also, it has been shown that the overall mass transfer is augmented by increased acid flow rate [15].

texts.text: 3.1.2. Effect of initial feed concentration, feed/stripping volume ratio, and chemical nature of acid stripping solution

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The results on the influence of the initial NH3  feed concentration, initial volume ratio (feed/acid), and type of acid stripping solution are shown in Figs. 6 and 7. Fig. 6 illustrates the N (%) recovery in the acid solution versus time for two levels of NH3 concentration.
texts.text: The results on the influence of the initial NH3  feed concentration, initial volume ratio (feed/acid), and type of acid stripping solution are shown in Figs. 6 and 7. Fig. 6 illustrates the N (%) recovery in the acid solution versus time for two levels of NH3 concentration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The initial concentration of NH3  in the feed solution significantly affects the efficiency, so that larger N (%) recovery in the acid solution was obtained at a high initial NH3 concentration. Thus, the values of N (%) in the acid solution were 8.0 and 4.4% (w/w) at around 5.0 and 2.4 gNH3L -1 initial concentrations, respectively. The results obtained here are in good agreement with those reported in previous studies [55 -57].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to the initial NH3 concentration, the initial volume ratio between the feed and the acid stripping tanks also plays an important role in PMP-HF-LLMC performance. The volumes of feed solution were 60 L and 10 L, while the volume of acid stripping solutions (0.4 M HNO3 and  0.4  M  H3PO4)  was  fixed  at  1.0 L  to  produce  NH4NO3 and (NH4)2HPO4/(NH4) H2PO4 as ammonium salts. It is noteworthy that the feed/acid volume ratio had a great and direct impact on the concentration of N-liquid fertiliser: the volume ratio of 60 culminated in 2 -4 times more concentrated liquid fertiliser in comparison to that obtained at a volume ratio of 10 (see Fig. 6). Vecino et al. [14] and Licon et al. [28]  considered  the  effect  of  volume  ratio  of  feed  and  acid  (HNO3) stripping (120 and 5) on NH3  removal and salt product concentration using  the  same  HF-LLMC  at  a  low  level  of  feed  concentration  (1.7 gNH3 L -1 ). It was proved that a volume ratio of 5 resulted in 98% NH3 removal compared to 85% for a volume ratio of 120. However, a later experiment (with a volume ratio of 120) produced fertiliser with a 24fold higher concentration (0.2 vs 4.9 w/w of N-fertiliser) than that of the former, which is good agreement with the current results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The type of acid (H3PO4  and HNO3) was studied to evaluate their effects on the quality of the N-liquid fertiliser. A single-nutrient fertiliser (N-fertiliser) could be obtained when HNO3 was used as the acid in the stripping solution, whereas a multi-nutrient compound fertiliser (N-P fertiliser) is produced when H3PO4  is used [26]. A multi-nutrient fertiliser  has  several  advantages,  including  fewer  by-products  and  good physical properties. It was also clear that H3PO4 resulted in considerably better performance compared to HNO3 (see Fig. 6 and Table 3) due to the higher N% in the final products for both high and low NH3  concentrations. HNO3  is a stronger acid than H3PO4; thereby the removal efficiency was different from that observed for H3PO4 [52]. To conclude, high NH3 feed concentration at high volume ratio with H3PO4 as stripping  solution  resulted  in  the  production  of  fertiliser  with  desirable qualities. Table 3 collates the results obtained by the Q-A60 module in NH3  recovery ratio, mass transfer coefficient, concentration of ammonium salts produced and water flux. As observed, initial NH3 feed concentration and feed volume had a major impact on N-fertiliser quality and time of experiment.

texts.text: 3.2. Monitoring the chemical stability of PMP-HF-LLMC and membrane pore wetting by spectroscopic measurements with UV-Vis absorption and 2D-EEM fluorescence spectroscopy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The chemical stability of the HFs and adhesive materials used in the production of the LLMC and pore-wetting events are difficult processes to monitor, so an autopsy of the module was carried out. Two spectroscopic techniques were used to measure the presence of potential byproducts generated during the filtration stage and to see their potential migration between fluid feed circuits and acid sides as a surrogate of the potential events of pore  wetting. During  operation,  organic component-based  by-products  are  expected  to  accumulate  on  the membrane surface but not transported through the membrane as they cannot be converted to the gaseous form; they could only be transported if pore-wetting events take place [58]. Dissolved organic matter (DOM; e.g., degradation by-products) leads to fouling but also impacts detrimentally on membrane features, which can result in membrane wetting. This phenomenon is generally seen at the feed/membrane interface due to the presence of molecular interactions between the gas, liquid, and solid  phases  [59].  Specifically,  membrane  wetting  is  defined  as  the penetration of liquids into the pores of the membrane. In the case of HFLLMC, this occurrence of membrane wetting could be related to the loss of membrane hydrophobicity leading to drops in flux and declines in permeation quality. The main factors influencing membrane wettability are the chemistry of membrane material and the geometry of the pores together with the surface tension of the aqueous medium and its interactions with  the membrane [60,61]. Water breakthrough pressure has been considered as an important criterion in characterising membrane wettability [62]. In HF-LLMC process with polar feed and stripping  solutions,  neither  must  penetrate  the  pores.  The  separation efficiency and durability of the HF-LLMC are ensured unless the pores are wetted. Therefore, the detection of the presence of DOM in the feed and acid streams is imperative to see whether they are transferred from the feed side to the stripping side in order to understand the occurrence of pore wetting. The changes in UV spectra at the wavelength range where DOM is expected (i.e., 190 -300 nm) at the beginning and end of the  NH3  recovery  experiments  for  both  feed  and  stripping  sides  are shown in Figure S2a and b , respectively. As observed, the absorbances shown in the UV-Vis spectra of the initial and final feed streams are very similar. The UV-Vis spectra are associated with the inorganic species used for preparing the feed (NaOH and NH3) and acidic streams (HNO3 or H3PO4). Spectra of the acidic streams where the liquid fertiliser is formed show an increase in absorbance due to the increased concentrations of nitrate and phosphate. Along with this, a slow decrease in the absorbance at low wavelengths in the feed side is observed due to the reduction in NH3  concentration. The relevant information from both spectra is the absence of any additional band due to the presence of potential organic by-products with expected absorbance peaks above 240 nm. Then, those at very low levels (e.g., < µ gL -1 ) were not detected by UV spectroscopy if they were present in the samples. Therefore, no organic  compounds  were  detected  in  either  HF-LLMC  liquid  circuit, indicating good chemical stability of the module under the basic and acidic conditions used. Additionally, no presence of new bands associated with nitrate and phosphate ions were detected in the feed stream, indicating that pore wetting did not occur during the experiments.

texts.orig: The type and composition of DOM can be classified using the information in the 2D-EEM fluorescence spectra. Fluorescence peaks can be characterised  by  emission/excitation  zones  with  higher  fluorescence intensity values. There are five distinct zones in 2D-EEM fluorescence spectra  (based  on  consistent  excitation  and  emission  wavelength boundaries;  Fig.  9)  which  can  be  related  to  the  five  main  groups  of fluorescent compounds [65], i.e., zone I: aromatic protein I (emission 280 -330  nm;  excitation  200 -250  nm);  zone  II:  aromatic  protein  II (emission 330 -380 nm; excitation 200 -250 nm); zone III: fulvic-acidlike (emission 380 -550 nm; excitation 200 -250 nm); zone IV: soluble microbial by-product-like (emission 280 -380 nm; excitation 250 -400 nm);  and  zone  V:  humic-acid-like  (emission  380 -550  nm;  excitation 250 -400). Fig. 9 (a -c) shows high fluorescence intensity in zones III and V, which implies that fulvic acid-like and humic acid-like compounds constitute the main DOM in the current samples. The feed water after 26 h continuous operation (Fig. 9 (c) ) showed the presence of low levels of DOM in the feed tank according to the measured emission fluorescence intensity in zones III and V during the PMP-HF-LLMC process [66]. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 9 (d -f) , the results indicated that no organic compound was detected in the permeate side at the end of the HF-LLMC process, which means that the small amount of fluorescent organic compound in the feed side did not transfer to the stripping side, indicating that no pore-wetting events had occurred during the filtration experiments. So the 2D-EEM fluorescence results were consistent with the UV -Vis results in that no pore wetting took place during the experiments using PMP-HF-LLMC.

texts.orig: of  NH3  removal/recovery.  Recently,  different  module  manufacturers have engaged in modifying the characteristics of HF-LLMCs as a challenging issue to reach high efficiency in NH3  removal and recovery. In this regard, the more relevant studies are summarised in Table 4. According to the data released from the Austrian Environment Agency [67] and Fertiberia [68], the standard N% for commercial fertiliser should be in the range of 15 -32% as a single nutrient. It could be observed that PMP-HF-LLMC has shown promising results regarding the production of N-liquid fertiliser compared to the other reported HF-LLMCs [2,8,26 -28] in which an NH3 recovery rate of 92% and N-liquid fertiliser composition of around 8.0% (N-NH4 + ) was achieved by employing H3PO4 as stripping solution with flow rates in the feed and acid solutions of 450 and 770 mL min 1 , respectively.
texts.text: of  NH3  removal/recovery.  Recently,  different  module  manufacturers have engaged in modifying the characteristics of HF-LLMCs as a challenging issue to reach high efficiency in NH3  removal and recovery. In this regard, the more relevant studies are summarised in Table 4. According to the data released from the Austrian Environment Agency [67] and Fertiberia [68], the standard N% for commercial fertiliser should be in the range of 15 -32% as a single nutrient. It could be observed that PMP-HF-LLMC has shown promising results regarding the production of N-liquid fertiliser compared to the other reported HF-LLMCs [2,8,26 -28] in which an NH3 recovery rate of 92% and N-liquid fertiliser composition of around 8.0% (N-NH4 + ) was achieved by employing H3PO4 as stripping solution with flow rates in the feed and acid solutions of 450 and 770 mL min 1 , respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Several  parameters  play  essential  roles  in  the  quality  of  N-liquid fertiliser produced by NH3 recovery. Thus, structural properties (active surface area, porosity, pore diameter and so on), transport properties (e. g., mass transfer coefficients and water and NH3  flux) and operational conditions such as (feed/acid) volume ratio and initial NH3 concentration should be scrutinised. It is worth pointing that the highest value of Km obtained in this study using high initial NH3 concentration was about (2.9 ± 0.2) × 10 7  ms 1 , while that of for PP-HF-LLMC was reported (8.8 ± 1.7) × 10 -7  ms 1 . This could be due to the lower porosity of PMP membrane forming a barrier against the diffusion of NH3 molecules.

texts.orig: As mentioned before, the initial NH3  concentration in the feed and feed/acid volume ratio are two vital parameters affecting the quality of N-liquid fertiliser. The N%-liquid fertiliser and water transport properties of PMP-HF-LLMC and other recent works in this field are compared in Fig. 10 (a) and (b) .

The N%-liquid fertiliser and water transport properties of PMP-HF-LLMC and other recent works in this field are compared in Fig. 10 (a) and (b) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally,  PMP-HF-LLMCs working  at  a  high  initial  NH3  concentration of about 5.0 g L -1 with the feed/acid volume ratio set at 60 have indicated acceptable water transport properties along with a percentage of N in the final liquid fertilisers that are located in the range of global industrial N% (w/w). As can bee seen in Fig. 10(a) the present study resulted in the production of fertiliser with almost 8.0 % N content using H3PO4  as  stripping  solution,  while  PP-HF-LLMC  in  previous  studies demonstrated similar results [26]. From another point of view, water flux within the membrane is another influential parameter that should be  considered  in  HF-LLMCs,  since  it  is  always  present  in  membrane contactor processes. Moreover, it is essential to equalise the mass balance of the fertiliser formed on the acid stripping side.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According to Fig. 10 (b) , the measured water flux in the current study was one order of magnitude lower than used for PP-HF-LLMCs in previous works [26]. It seems that the Q-A60 module containing PMP as a hydrophobic membrane and possessing a higher active surface area (7 m 2 )  compared  to  other  membranes,  along  with  multitude  of  fibres housed in the module, provides fewer and smaller pathways for water molecules to diffuse through the membrane, hence reducing the water flux through the PMP in comparison to PP-HF-LLMCs (JW - PMP = 0 . 004 ( kg m 2 h 1 ) < JW - PP = 0 . 04 ( kg m 2 h 1 ) ).  It  is  clear  that  the water flux increases continuously because the increase in vapour pressure difference with the reduction of feed NH3 concentration provides a greater  driving  force  for  mass  transfer.  In  addition,  PMP-HF-LLMC showed a high NH3 recovery percentage and a great role in preserving the  environment.  The  8.0  %  N  in  liquid  fertiliser  did  not  meet  the mentioned standards but the obtained results for water transport across the  membrane  were  significantly  promising.  However,  it  seems  that further efforts should be made to increase N% content. Various types of integrated  and/or  hybrid  systems  such  as  HF-LLMC  /  Electrodialysis (ED) and other operating conditions incuding pH, other type of acid (e. g.,  H2SO4)  and  temperature  can  be  considered  as  solutions  to  reach higher levels of N% in liquid or multi-nutrient fertilisers [14].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two new LLMCs using PMP-HF membranes with surface skin structure reported good performance indicators in both NH3  recovery and water  flux  through  the  membrane  using  H3PO4 and  HNO3  as  acid stripping solutions. The hydrophobic nature of these membranes provides the selective permeation of NH3(g)  molecules across the hollowfibre pores to react with acid solutions to produce ammonium salts. It was  concluded  that  the  performance  of  the  Q-type  PMP-HF-LLMC module was better than S-type fibres in terms of N% recovered in the acid tank, shorter operating times and higher JNH3  due to the higher porosity of the surface skin layer. Although the promising results for the less porous active layer (S-type fibre) in the A60 module indicated that the process applied produced a higher recovery percentage, the Q-A60 module managed to produce more concentrated N-liquid fertiliser towards reaching the global N% (w/w) range, which proved its superior applicability  compare  to  other  commonly  used  HF-LLMC  systems. Additionally,  the  quality  and  productivity  of  N-liquid  fertiliser  were dependent on the acid type, where H3PO4  showed considerably better performance than HNO3, as seen by the higher N% in the final products at  both  high  and  low  NH3 concentrations.  The  single  and  multicomponent fertilisers produced using H3PO4  or HNO3  stripping solutions  not  only  serve  agriculture  but  also  support  circular  economy concepts. Moreover, the study of performance and the economic analysis of scaling-up NH3 recovery from domestic wastewater can be promising for the future of HF-LLMC practice.

Additionally,  the  quality  and  productivity  of  N-liquid  fertiliser  were dependent on the acid type, where H3PO4  showed considerably better performance than HNO3, as seen by the higher N% in the final products at  both  high  and  low  NH3 concentrations.
